intro: Second-person framing. Mention that each task is a small function that calls a real Jetpack DataStore API, with a failing test to turn green, run from the gutter in Android Studio or with ./gradlew :datastore-prefs:test --tests "*ClassName".

tasks.title: createStoreAndWriteCount - build a Preferences DataStore and write a typed key
tasks.detail: Use PreferenceDataStoreFactory.create pointed at context.preferencesDataStoreFile(name) to build the store, then write value into it under COUNT_KEY with edit {}.
tasks.hint: val store = PreferenceDataStoreFactory.create { context.preferencesDataStoreFile(name) }; store.edit { it[COUNT_KEY] = value }; return store
tasks.verify: T1CreateAndWriteTest is green: the written value reads back correctly, and two differently-named stores don't share data.

tasks.title: readThemeOrDefault - fall back when a key is missing
tasks.detail: Reading a key that was never written returns null. Read THEME_KEY and fall back to "light" when it's missing.
tasks.verify: T2DefaultValueTest is green: an unset key returns the default, and a key that has been written returns its real value.

tasks.title: incrementAtomically - edit {} as an atomic read-modify-write
tasks.detail: Increment COUNT_KEY by reading and writing inside the same edit {} block, so it's atomic. Contrast with a naive read-then-write, which can drop updates under concurrency.
tasks.verify: T3AtomicIncrementTest is green: sequential increments add up, and 50 concurrent increments still land all 50, with no lost updates.

tasks.title: setName - a write, and the Flow that notices it
tasks.detail: Write NAME_KEY through edit {}. store.data is already a Flow that re-emits on every successful write, no extra plumbing needed.
tasks.verify: T4ObserveTest is green: the name is written, and a Flow collected from store.data emits again after the write with the new value.

tasks.title: safeData - catch IOException, fall back to empty Preferences
tasks.detail: store.data can throw IOException if the underlying file can't be read. Catch just that and emit emptyPreferences() instead; let any other exception keep propagating.
tasks.hint: return store.data.catch { e -> if (e is IOException) emit(emptyPreferences()) else throw e }
tasks.verify: T5CatchIOExceptionTest is green: an IOException is swallowed into emptyPreferences(), a different exception type still propagates, and real data passes through unaffected.

tasks.title: createStoreMigratingFrom - bring values in from SharedPreferences
tasks.detail: Build a DataStore that migrates existing values out of a legacy SharedPreferences file the first time it's opened, using the library's own SharedPreferencesMigration.
tasks.verify: T6MigrateFromSharedPreferencesTest is green: a value written to the legacy SharedPreferences file shows up in the DataStore, and an empty legacy file leaves the key unset.
